title: Show you heard them
concept: Acknowledge one specific detail before moving to the next question or topic.
skill_objective: User can make a conversation partner feel heard by responding to what they actually said rather than pivoting immediately.

concept_intro.text: Listening does not require a perfect response. Often, one small detail is enough: name what the person just said, react to it honestly, and then stay with that thread if you ask a question. A specific acknowledgement shows you were with them, while a fast pivot can make a conversation feel like two separate speeches.

example.setting: At a campus welcome lunch, Maya sits with a classmate who has also recently arrived. The classmate mentions that they are still figuring out the bus routes.

dialogue.speaker: Classmate
dialogue.text: I am still figuring out the bus routes. I took the wrong one yesterday and ended up near the stadium.

dialogue.speaker: Maya
dialogue.text: Near the stadium is quite a detour. I would have been checking the map every stop too. Which route were you trying to take?

dialogue.speaker: Classmate
dialogue.text: The number 12 to the library. I know the route now, at least.

bad.text: I need to find the library too. What classes are you taking?
bad.explanation: This jumps straight to the speaker’s own need and then to a new topic. It misses the classmate’s bus-route story, so the classmate has little sign that their detail landed.

better.text: Bus routes can be confusing when you are new.
better.explanation: This is kind and connected to what the classmate said. It is general, though, so it does not show that Maya noticed the specific detour or leave a clear path to continue the story.

best.text: Ending up near the stadium is a longer trip than you planned. Which bus were you trying to catch?
best.explanation: The reply picks up the stadium detour, a real detail from the classmate’s words. The follow-up stays on that detail, which makes the conversation feel attended to rather than redirected.

exercise.prompt: At the welcome lunch, a classmate says, “I am still figuring out the bus routes. I got off near the stadium yesterday.” Which reply shows you heard a specific detail?

options.text: I have been busy unpacking. Did you bring your family with you?
options.feedback: This changes to Maya’s situation and then asks a personal question. It does not acknowledge the bus-route detail.

options.text: Getting off near the stadium must have made the trip much longer. Which route were you aiming for?
options.feedback: Yes. It names the stadium detour and asks a follow-up that grows from the classmate’s own story.
This is broadly sympathetic, but it smooths over the specific experience instead of showing what you heard.

practice.scenario_setup: At a campus welcome lunch, a classmate says they are still figuring out bus routes and mentions one detail: they missed a stop, took the wrong route, or found a useful route to campus.
practice.user_task: Choose the reply that names the detail they gave before asking anything new. Then say which words in the reply show you stayed with their story.

parts.question: A classmate says, “I finally found the bus that goes straight to the science building, but I missed it twice this week.” Which detail should an attentive reply acknowledge?

options.text: They are sitting at a welcome lunch.
options.feedback: That is part of the setting, not the new information the classmate chose to share.

options.text: They found a direct bus but missed it twice.
options.feedback: Correct. This is the specific experience in their last turn. Naming either part of it shows you are responding to what they actually said.
Missing a bus does not tell you how they feel about public transport. Evidence spotting means using stated details, not adding an assumption.

parts.question: Which reply follows that bus-route detail most naturally?

options.text: Finding the direct bus is useful, even if catching it has been tricky. What time does it usually come?
options.feedback: Correct. It acknowledges both the useful route and the missed buses, then asks a question that remains on the same topic.

options.text: That reminds me, I am looking for a roommate. Do you know anyone?
options.feedback: This abruptly changes topics and asks the classmate to solve a separate problem.

options.text: You should set more alarms. What is your major?
options.feedback: This gives advice before acknowledging the experience, then pivots away. It can sound corrective rather than attentive.

parts.prompt: Read this detail from a new classmate: “I took the bus the wrong way and saw a part of the city I had not planned to visit.” Draft a response that points to the evidence you heard, then add one natural follow-up. Name the exact detail your response acknowledges.
parts.good_answer_demonstrates: A response that explicitly picks up the wrong-way bus trip or unexpected part of the city before asking a connected question, with a clear identification of the detail being acknowledged rather than a generic sympathy or abrupt pivot.
